lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
He spoke of me as united in faith with him; but what is his faith? Why are his books kept secret? I can meet any attack.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: These words are his own, he cannot deny them.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The very elegance of the style and the laboured mode of speech, and, surpassing all these, the Christian ‘simplicity’ which here appears, reveal the character of their author.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But there is a different phase of the matter: Eusebius, it seems, has depraved these books; and now my friend who accuses Origen, and who is so careful of my reputation, declares that both Eusebius and I have gone wrong together, and then that we have held correct opinions together, and that in one and the same work.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But he cannot now be my enemy and call me a heretic, when a moment before he has said that his belief was not dissonant from mine.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then, I must ask him what is the meaning of his balanced and doubtful way of speaking: “The Latin reader,” he says, “will find nothing here discordant from our faith.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What faith is this which he calls his?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it the faith by which the Roman Church is distinguished? or is it the faith which is contained in the works of Origen?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If he answers “the Roman,” then we are the Catholics, since we have adopted none of Origen’s errors in our translations.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But if Origen’s blasphemy is his faith, then, though he tries to fix on me the charge of inconsistency, he proves himself to be a heretic.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If the man who praises me is orthodox, he takes me, by his own confession as a sharer in his orthodoxy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If he is heterodox, he shews that he had praised me before my explanation because he thought me a sharer in his error.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: However, it will be time enough to reply to these books of his which whisper in corners and made their venomous attacks in secret, when they are published and come out from their dark places into the light, and when they have been able to reach me either through the zeal of my friends or the imprudence of my adversaries.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: We need not be much afraid of attacks which their author fears to publish and allows only his confederates to read.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then and not till then will I either acknowledge the justice of his charges, or refute them, or retort upon the accuser the accusations he has made: and will shew that my silence has been the result not of a bad conscience but of forbearance.
